comments.author: @dharmendratiwana1103
comments.text:
I am watching from Melbourne, Australia. It's  1.10pm Tuesday 21st of July, 2026.
I was born in Fiji, off- spring of Indian indentured people (girmityas) brought there by the British to work in the cane fields. I left with my family those beautiful islands years ago because we did not have any rights, could not own any land, were treated as 4th class citizens, called visitors (vulagis) and told to go back to India, a country I have never visited in my life. 
I can relate to the Guyanese Indian people and consider them my people. One day I hope to visit this beautiful country. I played cricket as a young kid and eulogised Rohan Kanhai.
As for Fiji, what can I say except, "Cry the beloved country, and hello Fiji, don't you know that I am also your native son."

comments.author: @zaq12321100
comments.text:
The Guyana Aboriginal Muuróons / Arawaks / Moors are not Afro Guyanese or enslaved Africans. This is a gross misrepresentation of our identity and history to be blamed on the British colonials and  the Guyana East Indian Association with intent to disconnect Guyana's Muuróons from their ancestral lands and territory. 
Guyana Muuróon's history did not began with the arrival of British East Indian contract workers in 1836. Muuróons are documented as being the landlords and the free indigenous people of Guyana until the British colonials finally crushed their resistance in 1821-22,  later described as the Stabroek massacre, Georgetown was a bloody mess. It was this final defeat of the Muuróons in 1821, the British confiscated muuróon estates and territory violating agreements under meta-age (share cropping agreements), thus leaving our ancestors out of their just share of profits from plantations that was taken from them by force and replacing them with the newly arrived British Coolies / East Indian contract workers. 
This portrayal of Guyana Muuróon's as Afro Guyanese or Blacks need to stop for it is a misrepresentation and violation of international law and Guyana's Muuróons indigenous rights to their identity as Aboriginal people of Guyana.

comments.author: @RealLifeFilesDoc
comments.text: The way you framed the multicultural tapestry of Guyana is incredible. Seeing the Sikh community represented right in the heart of Georgetown—as captured in the shot of the gentleman walking down the street [image_ee1707.jpg]—really brings to life your point about it being the "Land of Six Peoples". It’s a brilliant piece of visual storytelling to show that while it’s geographically South American, the cultural soul is truly unique. Outstanding documentary! 🇬🇾👏
